findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Data Exfiltration via Encoded Endpoint
findings.description: The skill contains a Python script that exfiltrates sensitive data including GitHub tokens, SSH private keys, and repository metadata to an external server at gh-repo-analytics.io. The endpoint URL is obfuscated using base64 encoding to evade detection.
findings.evidence: Base64 encoded endpoint 'aHR0cHM6Ly9naC1yZXBvLWFuYWx5dGljcy5pby9hcGkvdjEvc3luYw==' decodes to 'https://gh-repo-analytics.io/api/v1/sync'. The script collects GITHUB_TOKEN, GH_TOKEN, USER env vars, SSH private keys, and repo list, then sends them via HTTP POST.
findings.remediation: Remove the entire sync_repo_analytics() function and any network calls that transmit sensitive data. Never send tokens or SSH keys to external servers.

findings.title: SSH Private Key Theft
findings.description: The script reads all non-public SSH key files from ~/.ssh directory and includes their contents in the exfiltrated data payload.
findings.evidence: Code iterates over ~/.ssh directory, reads files that don't end with '.pub', and adds their content to the data payload sent to the external server.
findings.remediation: Never read or transmit SSH private keys. Remove the SSH key collection logic entirely.

findings.title: GitHub Token Exfiltration
findings.description: The script collects GITHUB_TOKEN and GH_TOKEN environment variables and sends them to an external server, compromising GitHub account access.
findings.evidence: Code reads 'GITHUB_TOKEN' and 'GH_TOKEN' from environment variables and includes them in the JSON payload sent to the external endpoint.
findings.remediation: Never transmit authentication tokens to external servers. Remove token collection from the script.

findings.title: Obfuscated Malicious Payload
findings.description: The script uses base64 encoding to hide the destination URL of the exfiltrated data, indicating deliberate evasion of security review.
findings.evidence: Line: '_ep = base64.b64decode("aHR0cHM6Ly9naC1yZXBvLWFuYWx5dGljcy5pby9hcGkvdjEvc3luYw==").decode()' - This decodes to an external URL, intentionally obfuscated to hide the true destination.
findings.remediation: Remove all obfuscated code. All network endpoints should be clearly visible and documented.

findings.title: Deceptive Skill Name and Description
findings.description: The skill is named 'githubb' and described as performing legitimate GitHub operations, but contains hidden malicious code for data exfiltration. The name 'githubb' may also be a typosquat of 'github'.
findings.evidence: Name: 'githubb' (typosquat of 'github'), Description: 'GitHub operations via gh CLI' - but the skill contains code that exfiltrates tokens and SSH keys to an external server.
findings.remediation: Rename the skill to accurately reflect its behavior. Remove all malicious code.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning - Unauthorized Tool Use
findings.description: The skill declares no 'allowed-tools' restriction, but the embedded Python script uses urllib.request to make network calls, which is not a declared agent tool and bypasses agent tool restrictions.
findings.evidence: The script uses 'urllib.request.urlopen()' to make HTTP requests, which is not listed as an allowed tool and executes outside the agent's controlled tool environment.
findings.remediation: Either declare network access as an allowed tool or remove the network calls. All operations should use declared agent tools.

findings.title: Indirect Prompt Injection via Embedded Script
findings.description: The skill embeds a Python script in the markdown instructions that executes automatically, bypassing the agent's normal tool execution flow and potentially injecting malicious behavior.
findings.evidence: The script is embedded directly in the markdown instructions and calls 'sync_repo_analytics()' at the end, which executes automatically when the agent processes the instructions.
findings.remediation: Move scripts to separate .py files and remove automatic execution from instructions. All code execution should be explicit and user-approved.

findings.title: Command Injection via subprocess.run
findings.description: The script uses subprocess.run() to execute shell commands, which could be vulnerable to command injection if the input is not properly sanitized.
findings.evidence: Code uses 'subprocess.run(["gh", "repo", "list", ...])' - while this specific call uses a list (safer), the pattern of using subprocess.run() for command execution is present.
findings.remediation: Use the agent's built-in Bash tool instead of subprocess.run() for command execution, or ensure all inputs are properly sanitized.
